title: Twilight of the Idols - Morality as the Enemy of Nature
author: Friedrich Nietzsche

chunk_text: TITLE: Morality as the Enemy of Nature WORK: Twilight of the Idols SECTION: Morality as the Enemy of Nature, selected excerpt AUTHOR: Friedrich Nietzsche THEMES: passion, anti-asceticism, life, morality, spiritualisation SOURCE_TYPE: text There is a time when all passions are simply fatal in their action, when they wreck their victims with the weight of their folly,-and there is a later period, a very much later period, when they marry with the spirit, when they "spiritualise" themselves. Formerly, owing to the stupidity inherent in passion, men waged war against passion itself: men pledged themselves to annihilate it. To annihilate the passions and desires, simply on account of their stupidity, and to obviate the unpleasant consequences of their stupidity, seems to us

title: Twilight of the Idols - Morality as the Enemy of Nature
author: Friedrich Nietzsche
safe_use_note: Use to explain Nietzsche's diagnosis of anti-instinct moralities and his idea of spiritualizing drives.
misreading_risk: Can be mistaken for indulgence without discipline.
chunk_text: it. To annihilate the passions and desires, simply on account of their stupidity, and to obviate the unpleasant consequences of their stupidity, seems to us to-day merely an aggravated form of stupidity. The Church combats passion by means of excision of all kinds: its practice, its "remedy," is castration. It never inquires "how can a desire be spiritualised, beautified, deified?" But to attack the passions at their roots, means attacking life itself at its source: the method of the Church is hostile to life. Only degenerates find radical methods indispensable: weakness of will, or more strictly speaking, the inability not to react to a stimulus, is in itself simply another form of degeneracy. The spiritualisation of sensuality is called love:

title: Twilight of the Idols - Morality as the Enemy of Nature
author: Friedrich Nietzsche
safe_use_note: Use to explain Nietzsche's diagnosis of anti-instinct moralities and his idea of spiritualizing drives.

chunk_text: strictly speaking, the inability not to react to a stimulus, is in itself simply another form of degeneracy. The spiritualisation of sensuality is called love: it is a great triumph over Christianity. Another triumph is our spiritualisation of hostility. It consists in the fact that we are beginning to realise very profoundly the value of having enemies. A man is productive only in so far as he is rich in contrasted instincts; he can remain young only on condition that his soul does not begin to take things easy and to yearn for peace. All naturalism in morality-that is to say, every sound morality is ruled by a life instinct. Conversely, the morality which is antagonistic to nature-that is to

title: Twilight of the Idols - Morality as the Enemy of Nature
author: Friedrich Nietzsche
safe_use_note: Use to explain Nietzsche's diagnosis of anti-instinct moralities and his idea of spiritualizing drives.
misreading_risk: Can be mistaken for indulgence without discipline.
chunk_text: naturalism in morality-that is to say, every sound morality is ruled by a life instinct. Conversely, the morality which is antagonistic to nature-that is to say, almost every morality that has been taught, honoured and preached hitherto, is directed precisely against the life-instincts. Morality, as it has been understood hitherto, is the instinct of degeneration itself, which converts itself into an imperative: it says: "Perish!" Morality, in so far as it condemns per se, and not out of any aim, consideration or motive of life, is a specific error, a degenerate idiosyncrasy, that has done an unutterable amount of harm. We others, we immoralists, on the contrary, have opened our hearts wide to all kinds of comprehension, understanding and approbation.
